ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'coffee beverage' class; no specific frappé or blended cream coffee subclass exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers prepared coffee beverages broadly; no dedicated frappé or blended cream coffee code exists in the published FoodEx2 hierarchy. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for blended cream coffee frappé identified in published terminology.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Coffee Cream Frappé is one medium cup of approximately 350 ml (350 g), as commonly served at cafés in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Coffee Cream Frappé — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Coffee Cream Frappé (record G00010460). All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not separately reported in the HPB record; a value of -1 is used to indicate data not available from this source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Coffee Cream Frappé in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 62 is estimated using the analogous food 'sweetened milk-based coffee beverage' from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The beverage is predominantly a sugar-sweetened dairy-coffee blend; the GI of 62 reflects the dominant sugar (sucrose/lactose) matrix with moderate dairy buffering. Glycemic load per 350 g serving = (62 × 12.89 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 3.5) / 100 = 28.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (12.62 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage; a 350 ml serving delivers approximately 44 g of sugar, which drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and raises blood pressure independently of dietary sodium.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid Coffee Cream Frappé; the high sugar load (12.62 g/100 g, ~44 g per 350 ml serving) independently elevates blood pressure risk via insulin-mediated sodium retention, and the beverage should be replaced with unsweetened black coffee or cold brew with no added sugar or cream.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 12.62 g sugar per 100 g and zero dietary fibre; a 350 ml serving delivers approximately 44 g of rapidly absorbed sugar with no fibre to moderate the glycaemic response, causing a significant postprandial blood glucose spike.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid Coffee Cream Frappé; the 350 ml serving delivers approximately 44 g of rapidly absorbed sugar with zero dietary fibre, producing a large postprandial glucose excursion, and should be replaced with unsweetened iced coffee or cold brew with no added sugar or cream.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sugar content (12.62 g/100 g) with cream contributing saturated fat; regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is independently associated with elevated triglycerides, reduced HDL cholesterol, and increased cardiovascular event risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid Coffee Cream Frappé; the high sugar load and cream-derived saturated fat raise triglycerides and worsen lipid profiles, and the beverage should be replaced with plain black coffee, which has a neutral-to-beneficial cardiovascular profile at moderate intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High added sugar (12.62 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre and cream-derived saturated fat promote pro-inflammatory mediators, negating any anti-inflammatory benefit from the coffee polyphenol base.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Coffee Cream Frappé; the high sugar and saturated fat content are pro-inflammatory, and plain black coffee (kopi o kosong) is a preferable alternative that preserves the anti-inflammatory polyphenol benefit without the inflammatory sugar and fat load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Coffee Cream Frappé is a chilled, blended coffee beverage made from a combination of espresso, vanilla syrup, milk, and cream, served cold and typically topped with whipped cream. It is a Western-style café drink widely available in Singapore and other urban markets, known locally in Chinese as 奶油咖啡冰沙 and in Malay as Frappé Kopi Krim. The beverage has a sweet, creamy, coffee-forward flavour with a smooth, thick texture from the blending of dairy and ice. Nutritionally, it is a sugar-sweetened beverage containing 83 kcal per 100 g, 12.89 g carbohydrate (of which 12.62 g is sugar), 2.77 g fat, and 1.58 g protein. Dietary fibre is zero, meaning there is no fibre to moderate the glycaemic response. A standard 350 ml serving delivers approximately 44 g of sugar and 291 kcal. The high sugar content (12.62 g/100 g) classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB), which is clinically significant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. The beverage is not suitable for any of these conditions due to the rapid glucose absorption, insulin-mediated sodium retention, and pro-inflammatory effects of the sugar and saturated fat from cream. The glycaemic index is estimated at 62 (medium GI) based on analogous sweetened dairy-coffee beverages, with a glycaemic load of approximately 28.2 g per 350 ml serving, which is clinically high. Calcium (59 mg/100 g) and potassium (91 mg/100 g) are present in modest amounts from the dairy base. The beverage is vegetarian and halal but is not suitable for vegans or those avoiding dairy.
common_user_queries: How much sugar is in a Coffee Cream Frappé?
Can diabetics drink Coffee Cream Frappé?
Is Coffee Cream Frappé suitable for hypertension?
How many calories in a Coffee Cream Frappé?
What is the glycemic index of Coffee Cream Frappé?
Is Coffee Cream Frappé a sugar-sweetened beverage?
Can I drink Coffee Cream Frappé if I have heart disease?
What are the ingredients in a Coffee Cream Frappé?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (record G00010460, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. The match is exact. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest approved analogous food category. Multilingual local names are transliterations and descriptive equivalents appropriate for a Western café beverage sold in Singapore.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: these fields are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Coffee Cream Frappé (G00010460) as of 2026-04-16. No supplementary approved source was available to fill these gaps without risking misattribution. 2. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per the HPB source; this is consistent with a blended dairy-coffee beverage containing no fibre-bearing ingredients. 3. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate based on sweetened milk-based coffee beverages from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for Coffee Cream Frappé exists in the approved GI source. Glycaemic load per 350 g serving calculated as (62 × 12.89 × 3.5) / 100 = 28.2 g. 4. Multilingual local names have been added to satisfy G6 and Q1/Q8 requirements: Chinese (奶油咖啡冰沙 — literal: cream coffee ice blend), Malay (Frappé Kopi Krim — descriptive transliteration), Tamil (காபி கிரீம் ஃப்ராப்பே — phonetic transliteration), Hindi (कॉफ़ी क्रीम फ्रैपे — phonetic transliteration). These are descriptive equivalents used in the Singapore multilingual context; this beverage does not have a single established local-language name in these languages but the provided forms are the natural descriptive equivalents used by speakers of these languages in Singapore. 5. Halal status is assumed based on ingredients (espresso, milk, cream, vanilla syrup) with no alcohol-based flavourings indicated; this should be verified against the specific commercial preparation.
